data.title: ConocoPhillips: Starting 2025 With Marathon
data.description:
Towards year-end 2024, I believed that shares of ConocoPhillips (NYSE:COP) were starting to reveal following a $22 billion stock purchase of Marathon Oil, which added high-quality and low-cost supply.

That deal, which was announced in spring of 2024, and followed a round of M&A across the sector, closed in November. The transaction makes that Conoco will see real growth in 2025, although less pronounced on a per-share basis due to the all-stock transaction structure.

That said, modest leverage, a low double-digit earnings multiple, all amidst relatively subdued energy prices creates a decent set-up for investors in the long haul.

Some Perspective

After its major peers embarked on major M&A efforts earlier in 2024, Conoco went after Marathon Oil in order to add significant reserves and production adjacent to own properties in areas like the Eagle Ford, the Bakken and the Delaware basin.

The deal for Marathon called for synergies of around $500 million, achievable in just one year post closing, as the company announced an ambitious and aggressive 34% increase in the dividend, payable at $3.12 per annum. In all fairness, there was some catching up to do, after these payouts were cut during the pandemic.

It was a bit hard to read into the pro forma implications after 2023 results showed a big pullback from strong 2022 results. Conoco´s sales fell 30% in 2023 to $58 billion, on which it still posted operating profits of $16 billion and net earnings of $11 billion, equal to $9 per share. This all was based on production of about 1.8 million barrels of oil-equivalent per day.

Conoco was valued at $147 billion in May 2024, when shares traded at $115 per share. The $22 billion deal for Marathon was equal to 15% of the own valuation at the time, while it would add nearly $7 billion in sales and about 400,000 barrels of oil-equivalent production per day. This implied an 11-12% contribution to pro forma revenues and 21% increase in production (due to lower realizations, read higher natural gas contributions of that same production base).

Shares were down to $95 by year-end, driven by some easements in the global political environment, an outlook for subdued demand and better energy policies (favoring production) in the US following the outcome of the US presidential election. This was visible in the marketplace as oil prices fell from $80 per barrel in May, to $70 per barrel by year-end.

Amidst lower realizations, I believed that the company was on track to earn close to $7 per share in 2024, this being based on annualised third quarter results. This resulted in a non-demanding 13 times earnings multiple based on oil prices, which were trading at relatively subdued levels.

The company guided for pro forma capital spending (of the combined entity) at $13 billion for 2025, sufficient to keep production flat, with depreciation charges seen around $12 billion.

I believed that pro forma earnings power of $7 per share might benefit in a modest fashion from synergies, yet it were oil prices in particular, and energy prices at large, which determine the earnings outlook for Conoco, and thereby the share price outlook. I considered oil prices around $70 per barrel to be relatively modest, certainly if we account for inflation seen in recent years, yet there were real headwinds related to ESG measures, pollution and the energy transition.

Despite these headwinds, it is restrained practices within the sector which are comforting, with strong balance sheets allowing for continued distributions to investors, as a >3% dividend yield in the case of Conoco was still relatively subdued.

Greater Insight Into 2025

Since the start of the year, oil prices have traded in a $70-$80 per barrel range, now having moved towards the lower end of the range as geopolitical tensions are coming down and concerns about the health of the economy are mounting.

By early February, Conoco posted relatively sound fourth quarter results, with adjusted earnings for the quarter coming in at $1.98 per share. The company ended the year with $19.2 billion in debt, with the Marathon deal closing just before year-end, contributing about a month to the annual results.

Aided by the contribution of Marathon, the company sees production for 2025 around 2.36 million barrels of oil-equivalent per day, plus or minus 20,000 barrels. Capital spending for 2025 is seen around $12.9 billion, seen up about one and a half a billion from the estimated depreciation expense, a bit worse than I anticipated towards year-end 2024.

All this looks quite reasonable if you ask me, although that some modest net capital investments are expected. Of course, dilution will be incurred, which makes it hard to reliably indicate what the impact of the Marathon deal on earnings per share is, yet I am comforted by the strong fourth quarter adjusted earnings number.

With 1.27 billion shares now trading at $97, the company's equity is granted a $123 billion equity valuation, and $142 billion enterprise valuation, basically below the absolute valuation when the deal with Marathon was announced last year!

A Tiny Divestment

As part of its program to deleverage, the company announced a bolt-on divestment. This is more portfolio optimization rather than a necessity, as net debt of around $19 billion lags considerably in ration to $23 billion in EBITDA reported in 2024 (which number furthermore only benefits from 1 month contribution from Marathon Oil).

The company soon delivered on another divestment, as it announced a $735 million deal to sell its interest in the Ursa and Europe Fields, as well as pipeline interest, to Shell, with its representative ownership interest representing production of some 8,000 barrels of oil-equivalent per day.

That is equal to about a third of a percent impact on total production (or about 0.3%) as the headline price is equal to about 0.5% of the pro forma enterprise valuation. This looks quite reasonable, without further details being released on the assets, but this is a small divestment in the grand scheme of things.

What Now?

The truth is that shares still trade at rather compelling levels. Based on current conditions, I think that earnings power might be trending closer to $8 per share, up from a previous estimate around $7 per share. Based on such a number, this yields a mere 12 times earnings multiple, with a management team being friendly to return capital to investors.

On the back of this story, shares continue to trade at very reasonable valuation multiples, given that net debt remains relatively modest, as the same applies for oil prices at large.

Trading at a low double-digit earnings multiple, with potential for earnings and cash flows to improve if oil prices recover from relatively modest levels (compared to price levels seen in the past decade) I still think that investors might look for outsized returns here over time.

data.title: Archrock: Politics Overshadows Business
data.description:
Archrock (AROC) is going to be reporting its earnings on Tuesday, February 25, 2025, after making what was widely regarded as a good acquisition. However, political considerations may overshadow the company prospects because there are rapidly spreading fears of a possible recession. Long time readers may remember that compressors were being returned to all of these companies in the industry back in 2020 during that industry downturn.

Last Article

Back in 2020 I wrote that the stock was probably a buy due to the low price of the stock and the fact that the industry would recover. Of course, it was hard to go wrong buying anything back in 2020.

Now, the long-term outlook for natural gas is actually quite bright with the increasing ability of North America to export natural gas along with some other demand growth ideas.

But the problem is that all of a sudden, the policies coming out of the nation's capital appear to me to be bringing on a recession. Companies do not do well during periods of upheaval, and right now there is plenty of that and more uncertainty.

That means there is a very good chance of a recession or worse, in my opinion. Tariff fears are one of the issues feared to cause a recession. Large government cutbacks would remove a lot of spendable money from the economy all at once. This and much more make the situation the polar opposite of the situation back in 2020 when a recovery from the COVID challenges was a nearly certain event.

Not only are the tax cuts at stake, but a possible trade war resulting from the tariff strategy is an issue as well. Even if the worst thing that happens is more tariffs, those tariffs in the modern era act as a tax increase. We have a continuing budget deficit to contend with. Elimination of that deficit likely means short-term pain, which is bad news for just about any company including this one.

Recent Acquisition

To show how fast things can change in this industry, the recent acquisition actually looked good when it was announced and completed. No one had the foggiest notion of the issues we would now be facing, despite what was said during the campaign.

If anything, there appears to be a general misunderstanding about what tariffs do and how they work. Far from being a weapon to bring about equalization or righting perceived inequalities, they have a very poor record of delivering the long-term results that one would hope for.

Basically, the company expanded right before trouble appeared. That can very easily happen in this industry. As long as the outlook for natural gas production continued along reasonable lines, there was really nothing to fear.

But what has now entered the picture is a potential decrease in natural gas production should a business recession or worse happen. Any investment here would assume that a recession would not happen. Right now, missing a recession appears to be a less likely situation that is getting far less likely.

Debt

The acquisition raised the debt levels as shown below:

Clearly, the debt ratio is a good deal more conservative than it was in the past. But the warning comes in the form of what happened in fiscal year 2020. Much of the equipment is contracted out, so the climb in the debt ratio from lower activity levels was not horrible. But it bears watching because the market is unlikely to accept more than a little higher debt ratio.

Furthermore, the company noted the advantages of younger equipment as part of the corporate presentation. But the portfolio of equipment to lease out ages when fewer purchases can be made during a cyclical downturn. That could present some challenges industrywide when a recovery happens (especially if a technology change results in a better "mousetrap").

Stock Price

For an industry that has long-lived contracts, but services a largely cyclical industry like oil and gas, the stock price is now sky-high.

The price-earnings ratio is absolutely screaming a warning. Back when I wrote my article in 2020, the company ran a cyclical loss for a bit. That means during the good times, a price earnings ratio in the single digits is indicated.

Back in 2020, the dividend was more than 5%. Whenever the dividend goes past 5%, it is likely the time to consider buying this stock. In contrast, the yield right now is much more typical of premium stock price pricing.

Now, supposedly, natural gas prices are due to recover and the ability to export natural gas should climb. However, as noted above, the political considerations may more than overshadow all the good things as they did back in 2020 when the COVID challenges caused a sizable oil production declined that later changed into very high commodity prices in 2022.

Voters thought that they voted for an inflation solution and better times ahead. Right now, the opposite appears to be more likely, in my opinion. It may not happen, but the growing possibility will affect stock prices.

In any event, the stock appears to be at least fully priced. Yet, the reasons for all that optimism are likely disappearing. The result is that this may well be one of many stocks about to head downward despite what is likely to be a very decent quarterly report in the near future. Mr. Market looks forward, and all Mr. Market sees right now is a lack of future clarity.

Summary

Archrock is probably one of the best run compressor companies that I follow. However, even a well-run company may find it hard to avoid what is now going on. It may therefore be wise to rate this company as a sell until the current situation clears up and some consistency returns. Cash may turn out to be king for a year or two.

The compressor market that this company occupies is one of the steadier performers of the oil and gas industry. But it is not immune to cyclical downturns despite the long-term nature of the leasing business.

I personally would like to believe that tariffs are a negotiating tool that in practical terms would never happen.

But the government cutbacks are so severe as to remove a considerable amount of demand from the economy. That part is now certain, just based upon actions already taken. Combine this with removing large numbers of illegal immigrants from key industries that depend upon them, and you have the creation of a major disruption underway. The actual follow through effects will have to be seen as they happen. But right now, the future looks rough. When that happens, then usually holding cash wins.

Risks

Right now, by far, is the risk surrounding the economy. Decisions are being made now that may well have unfavorable outcomes for years to come. Cutting back government at the current pace has a flow through effect that will likely shrink the private sector as well.

The negotiations using tariffs likewise carry the threat of a trading war or a recession. Tariffs on imports are a tax on our citizens and therefore act to decrease demand. Of all the problems facing the average citizen, this one is the least likely to happen. On the other hand, it should not be on the table to start with. We are now in the position of hoping that negotiations with our trading partners come to something more reasonable than all the words going back and forth right now.

The last of these is the government budget deficit. If that gets resolved, and we finally begin to tackle the government debt, then there will be pain first and likely better times ahead. But that "pain ahead" likely means it's time to raise cash.

The biggest worry for the industry is a prolonged period of compressor returns that result in low usage rates and of course quarterly losses. With all the uncertainty discussed before this, it is hard to tell what a reasonable future would be or what the balance sheet should look like to get through whatever lies ahead.

data.title: Chewy: No Reason To Expect Outperformance
data.description:
Nearly three years ago, back in June of 2022, one company that I decided to downgrade from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’ was Chewy (CHWY). For those not familiar with the business, it is a pet oriented firm that makes available to customers around 115,000 products and services through its digital platforms. This decision was based on certain challenges the company was experiencing, such as margin contraction and the fact that shares were looking pricey. Since that time, the stock has risen by another 32.4%. But that pales in comparison to the 47.8% upside achieved by the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

Fundamentally today, the company continues to do quite well. Revenue and cash flows are rising, and that trend is likely to continue for the foreseeable future. But shares are still expensive and that is true relative to similar firms as well. The good news is that the firm does have a robust balance sheet. But that's not enough to compensate for how expensive the stock currently is. Because of this, I have decided to keep it rated a ‘hold’ for now.

Of course, the picture can change as new data comes in. It just so happens that, at some point in the not-too-distant future, management will be reporting financial results for the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. We don't yet have a date for that. But last year, that date fell on March 20th. So in all likelihood, by the end of next month, we will see something come through. The good news is that analysts currently believe that results will, for the most part, come in better year over year. But that's not enough in my view to justify an upgrade.

Growth continues

Before I get into the most recent data, I think it would be helpful to talk about how the company performed from 2022 to 2023. During this window of time, revenue for the company grew by 10.2% from $10.12 billion to $11.15 billion. This increase was driven mostly by the consumables that the company sells. Overall revenue for that category jumped 12.2% from $7.15 billion to $8.01 billion. This was because of higher spending per active customer. During this time, net sales per active customer jumped from $496 to $555. This was offset, to some extent, by a decrease in the number of active customers from 20.41 million to 20.08 million.

Even though the customer base for the company declined, the firm did see strong activity from what it calls its ‘Autoship’ customers. These are subscription based customers that have orders automatically placed, paid for, and delivered. This is nothing less than a big source of recurring revenue for the company that, in theory, reduces the overall risk for investors. Year over year, revenue associated with these customers grew a whopping 14.7% from $7.41 billion to $8.49 billion. This means that these customers went from accounting for 73.2% of the company's revenue to accounting for 76.2%.

This growth in revenue did not bring with it higher profits. Actual net income dropped from $49.9 million to $39.6 million. However, adjusted net profits grew from $226.5 million to $296.2 million. I don't actually like using adjusted net profits in some circumstances. And this is one of those. Management makes this figure more akin to cash flow by adding back share-based compensation. At the end of the day, employees would need to be paid this compensation in some capacity. So I don't believe adding it back makes sense for determining overall profits. Other profitability metrics saw improvements as well. Operating cash flow rose from $349.8 million to $486.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $345.3 million to $416.7 million. There is also EBITDA. Year over year, it grew from $306.7 million to $368.1 million.

For 2024, we do currently have data covering through the first nine months. During this time, revenue for the company totaled $8.61 billion. That's up 3.5% compared to the $8.32 billion reported the previous year. The firm continued to see a decline in active customers from 20.27 million to 20.16 million. However, net sales per active customer contracted from $544 to $567. Autoship revenue continued to impress, climbing from 76.1% of overall revenue to 78.7%. This translated to a year-over-year increase in sales associated with those operations of 7%. That meant a rise in revenue from $6.33 billion to $6.78 billion.

Net profits during this time improved drastically from $7.7 million to $370 million. Much of this was because of a $215.6 million tax benefit that the company generated in the first nine months of 2024. On an adjusted basis, net profits improved from $216 million to $326.8 million. Operating cash flow moved up slightly from $386.7 million to $388.8 million. That if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a pretty sizable increase from $315.6 million to $430.9 million. And lastly, EBITDA for the company grew from $281.6 million to $446 million.

From a purely fundamental perspective, I would say that things are looking up. And when it comes to the rest of this year, management believes things will be fine. They currently expect revenue for the year as a whole to be between $11.79 billion and $11.81 billion. That would imply sales for the final quarter of between $3.18 billion and $3.20 billion. For the year as a whole, they also expect an EBITDA margin of between 4.6% and 4.8%. At the midpoint of all of these numbers, we would get a reading of $554.6 million. If we assume that adjusted operating cash flow will rise at the same rate, this would give us a reading for the year of $568.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see what this means for the valuation of the company. To be honest with you, shares look very expensive. Relative to other pet or animal oriented firms, as shown in the table below, the stock is a bit closer to being fairly valued. On both a price to operating cash flow basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, I found that three of the five companies ended up being cheaper than Chewy is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Chewy 27.1 26.9
Freshpet (FRPT) 45.3 52.7
Central Garden & Pet Company (CENT) 6.5 10.1
IDEXX Laboratories (IDXX) 39.8 29.9
Elanco Animal Health (ELAN) 10.7 7.1
Zoetis (ZTS) 24.3 19.5

Naturally, this picture can change with new data. As I mentioned at the start of the article, sometime in March, management should be reporting financial results for the final quarter of 2023. The current expectation from analysts is that sales will be around $3.20 billion. Should this come to fruition, it would translate to a year-over-year growth of 13.2%. This would also be at the high end of the growth that management forecasted for the quarter. In all likelihood, this increase will be driven by higher revenue from existing customers, with some of the growth offset by a decline in active customers. That is certainly something that investors should continue to pay attention to. Declining customers in moderation is acceptable. But it is becoming something of a trend for the company.

Analysts do believe that profitability will come in mixed. The current expectation is for earnings per share of only $0.05. That would represent a drop from the $0.07 in earnings the company reported for the final quarter of 2023. That would translate to a drop in net profits from $31.9 million to $21.3 million. On an adjusted basis, however, analysts believe that earnings per share will climb from $0.18 to $0.20. This would translate to an increase from $80.3 million in adjusted net profits to $85.3 million. In the table above, you can see other profitability metrics for the final quarter of 2023. If revenue and adjusted profits are both on the rise, I suspect these should increase as well. It is good news that management believes that EBITDA for the quarter will come in at between $96.3 million and $97.2 million. Even at the low end, that would be above the $86.5 million reported for 2023.

Those who follow my work closely might be perplexed that I am not rating Chewy a ‘sell’ instead of a ‘hold’. After all, the stock is quite expensive on an absolute basis. Well, the relative valuation here certainly helps a little bit. But I don't put a lot of weight on that. The overall growth in revenue and cash flows helps as well, as does the fact that the company has no debt and enjoys a net cash balance of $507.5 million. However, there are two other reasons why I am more neutral than I am bearish. The first of these is the large percentage of its revenue that is recurring in nature and the fact that the percent of revenue coming from recurring sources is growing. This, to me, certainly reduces the risk profile for shareholders.

Another contributor is the fact that the overall industry in which the company operates is significant in size. This data is a bit old since it was from the firm's 2023 investor day presentation. However, they did not have comparable data back in 2024. According to them, the US pet market is worth about $144 billion. $87 billion of that involves pet food and supplies. Another $47 billion involves pet health, with the remaining $10 billion falling under the pet services category. Only about 36% of overall revenue in this industry on the retail side involves online sales. And of that space, Chewy boasts a 12% stake. Further moves toward all things digital should prove beneficial for the company. And there's also the fact that, from 2023 through 2027, management was expecting the industry to grow at an annualized rate of between 5% and 7%. These factors, at least to me, justify a slightly more generous assessment of the business. But only barely.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I will admit that I'm not a huge fan of Chewy from an investment perspective. But I do think the company is doing a fine job. Revenue and cash flows are growing nicely, and that trend is likely to continue. The company has a robust balance sheet and increased spending from remaining customers is promising. I do think investors should continue to watch the decline in active customers. Furthermore, those who are price conscious should certainly steer clear of this opportunity. But at the end of the day, I do think that settling on a soft ‘hold’ rating is appropriate.

data.title: Why AbbVie Remains A Strong Buy For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
Since the publication of my article "Top 4 Immunology Stocks Poised For Growth In 2025," AbbVie's stock price (NYSE:ABBV) has risen by over 17%.

Why do I continue to cover AbbVie with a 'Strong Buy' rating?

The first reason is that the North Chicago-based company posted financial results for the fourth quarter of 2024 at the end of January, which again beat my and Wall Street analysts' expectations, thanks to the strong performance of its immunology and neuroscience franchises.

Secondly, in the little over a month that has passed since the publication of my article, AbbVie has pleased me not only with its progress in developing its pipeline of product candidates for the treatment of cancer and degenerative disorders but also with its anti-infective portfolio.

So, on February 7, 2025, the FDA approved Emblaveo in combination with metronidazole for the treatment of patients with complicated intra-abdominal infections.

Also, a week later, it signed a deal with Xilio Therapeutics to develop new immunotherapies for the treatment of various types of cancer.

Dear Seeking Alpha readers, let's continue to delve into AbbVie's latest achievements and failures with me.

AbbVie's financial position continued to improve in Q4 2024

AbbVie's revenue was about $15.1 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024, up 5.6% year-on-year and 4.4% quarter-on-quarter.

Meanwhile, it beat the consensus estimate in seven of the last eight quarters, even though its oncology franchise's sales growth rate was unimpressive.

So, its total sales amounted to about $1.69 billion in Q4 2024, increasing by 12.1% year-on-year and only by 0.2% quarter-on-quarter.

Then, dear Seeking Alpha readers, you may have the first question, namely, how was AbbVie able to beat my expectations and Wall Street analysts?

In short, the growth in revenue and strengthening of its balance sheet was caused by the continued extremely high demand for its key medications for the treatment of autoimmune and neurological disorders.

Let's continue to drill down into the table above.

Its immunology franchise sales reached about $7.3 billion in the fourth quarter, up 4.9% year over year and 3.5% quarter over quarter.

More interesting dynamics can be seen after I conducted a comparative analysis of its sales year-on-year.

You, dear Seeking Alpha readers, may have two logical questions.

Why is its revenue growth rate so low? Why does AbbVie's share price continue to grow?

First, I will answer the first question. The answer to this question lies in the sharp drop in Humira sales due to the negative impact of its biosimilars and AbbVie's effort to switch patients from it to Rinvoq and Skyrizi.

Second, despite this, sales of Skyrizi and Rinvoq reached $3.78 billion and $1.83 billion, respectively, beating my high-end expectations and completely offsetting the damage from the fall in demand for Humira.

Also, on the Q4 earnings call, AbbVie's CEO Robert Michael pleasantly surprised me by announcing a raise in the 2027 sales outlook for these two blockbusters.

Based on this strong momentum, we now expect Skyrizi and Rinvoq to exceed more than $31 billion of combined sales in 2027, which is $4 billion above the guidance we provided last year.

We are seeing strong performance across all of their approved indications, especially in IBD. And we see several tailwinds that will support growth into the next decade, including healthy immunology market growth, strong share capture given best-in-class profiles, continued robust market access, and momentum from new indications, such as the recent launch of Skyrizi in UC, as well as the potential for five new indications for Rinvoq over the next few years.

Let's continue.

Earnings per share [EPS], which is my preferred financial metric, has also not disappointed, even as AbbVie's R&D expenses have continued to rise in recent years, mainly due to costly late-stage clinical development programs for Rinvoq, elezanumab and epcoritamab.

Overall, its non-GAAP earnings per share [non-GAAP EPS] was $2.16, down 22.6% from Q4 2024, primarily due to the acquisitions of Aliada Therapeutics and Nimble, which allowed AbbVie to rejuvenate and strengthen its portfolio of product candidates developing to treat autoimmune and central nervous system diseases.

Thanks to the data provided through Seeking Alpha, it is possible to look a little into the future and assess how certain financial metrics of AbbVie will change.

So, Wall Street analysts are forecasting non-GAAP EPS to be in the range of $2.48 to $2.7 in the first quarter of 2025, implying 9.1% year-over-year growth. By comparison, I expect it to be $2.61, which is above the midpoint of the above range due to stronger sales of Vraylar, Ubrelvy and Skyrizi.

Has AbbVie made progress in improving its business profitability?

The answer is more yes than no.

AbbVie's gross profit was about $10.7 billion in Q4 2024, up about $680 million year-on-year. While its operating income margin reached 31.3%, up 2.4% quarter-on-quarter, firstly dispelling my concerns about the impact of increased competition in the immunology drugs market, and secondly, it was higher than its main competitor, namely Johnson & Johnson [operating income margin was 16.2%] (JNJ).

AbbVie's success in strengthening and rejuvenating its portfolio of experimental drugs

AbbVie has a strong position in the autoimmune disease therapeutics market, and even after launching more and more biosimilars to Humira, it continues to prove to me, among others, that it can exceed even the wildest expectations.

Another thing I like about AbbVie is its strategic focus on mainly only three therapeutic areas, namely immunology, neurology, and cancer. This R&D policy is somewhat similar to that of Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO), which are leaders in the obesity and diabetes market.

What are the advantages of such a policy?

Well, I highlight several.

First, AbbVie is extremely flexible in marketing because its medications are prescribed by doctors who treat similar conditions. Moreover, I believe that due to Humira's 15+ years of dominance, it has a large patient base and a more than well-established connection in the medical community.

Second, with a strong focus on the immunology market, which, as I discussed in the article "Top 4 Immunology Stocks Poised for Growth in 2025," is valued at over $190 billion in 2028, this allows AbbVie to develop "gold standards" before its blockbusters lose exclusivity.

These next-generation drugs often have competitive advantages in efficacy and safety profiles relative to those being developed by competitors with a much broader portfolio of product candidates. Of these competitors, I highlight several, including Pfizer (PFE) and GSK (GSK).

Thirdly, I believe there is a so-called synergistic effect between Rinvoq and Skyrizi, which is that they almost do not compete with each other but rather complement each other, covering different drug classes and groups of patients suffering from immune-mediated inflammatory diseases.

In simple words, Rinvoq and Skyrizi are like "one Humira," only offering patients more flexibility in treatment and also having significantly better efficiency, as demonstrated by numerous studies, which I mentioned in previous articles.

Fourthly, unlike those Big Pharma players who prefer the strategy of "the bigger and more diversified the portfolio, the better," AbbVie, having only the most promising experimental drugs in its portfolio, allows it to reduce the risk of capital misallocation and, as a result, have a relatively high EBIT margin, which, in my opinion, will only grow in the next five years.

Moreover, I believe that having only a few product candidates with unique and complex mechanisms of action that have already shown promising results in preclinical studies relative to AbbVie's already FDA-approved drugs allows them to be a "safeguard" in case of loss of exclusivity of one or another blockbuster, as well as to be potentially approved for a wide range of diseases. As a consequence, this also reduces the development time since the collection of clinical data for regulatory authorities begins with Phase 2.

Finally, in my personal experience, doctors prefer to deal with medications that have been on the market for a long time because of their predictable effects on patients, as well as a well-studied safety profile, that is, it is clear how to control side effects that arise.

Now, I want to go back a little.

AbbVie began an aggressive M&A push four years ago to strengthen its presence in fast-growing cancer and CNS therapeutics markets, which I estimate will start to pay off for the company and investors in 2026, including through the launch of tavapadon if it is approved by regulators for the treatment of Parkinson's disease.

But of course, I don't end there.

It's time, dear Seeking Alpha readers, to discuss its pipeline of experimental drugs.

From its list of product candidates, I highlight lutikizumab [hidradenitis suppurativa, ulcerative colitis, and atopic dermatitis], tavapadon [Parkinson's disease], ABBV-453 [R/R multiple myeloma and chronic lymphocytic leukemia], etentamig [multiple myeloma and AL amyloidosis], ABBV-400 [colorectal cancer, solid tumors, and lung cancer], and ABBV-RGX-314 [wet AMD and diabetic retinopathy ], which, in my estimation, will not only further increase the interest of retail and institutional investors, but also become "cash cows" if approved.

Overall, as I have already indicated in previous articles, my conclusions are based on the already demonstrated results in Phase 1/2 or Phase 3 clinical trials, but also on comparisons with competitors.

Risks

It's time to discuss the key risks associated with AbbVie, which I believe every Seeking Alpha reader should consider.

Takeaway

On January 31, AbbVie announced financial results for the fourth quarter of 2024, which I was eagerly awaiting because I wanted to see how well Robert Michael was doing in developing the immunology franchise and, more importantly, his ability to reach new heights in the development of the oncology portfolio.

And he did not disappoint me.

Also, the growth of dividend payments over the past 5 years by 7.5% and the fact that its total debt has fallen by more than $6 billion over the past 9 months [at the end of March 2024 it was $74.4 billion, and at the end of December it was $68.3 billion] for me are important investment theses.

However, this is not all.

The additional factor, thanks to which I believe that AbbVie remains an attractive stock for investors, is the expected high growth rates of its EPS until 2029.

So, I expect its non-GAAP EPS to rise to $18.5 in 2029. This value is above the median analyst range [$15.2 to $21.24], as I discussed in the article "AbbVie: Upgrading To 'Strong Buy' After Pullback," due to my more optimistic sales expectations for Skyrizi, Rinvoq, Elahere, and Duodopa.

Given AbbVie's current share price of $202.08, this corresponds to a P/E ratio of 10.92x, which is 41% lower than the sector average and 12% lower than its 5-year average. As a result, this is an additional factor that indicates that it is trading at a discount at the moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Fund performance

Institutional Class shares of Columbia Commodity Strategy Fund (MUTF:CCSZX) returned -0.21% for the fourth quarter ending December 31, 2024. The fund’s benchmark index, the Bloomberg Commodity Index, returned -0.45% for the same period.

Market overview

For the fourth quarter, the Bloomberg Commodities Index slid a marginal 0.45% following lackluster global demand for oil and a marginal decline in gold prices. China's persistent economic slowdown, characterized by sluggish construction, manufacturing and consumer activity, dampened oil consumption. Additionally, gold fell as a stronger dollar pushed the price per bullion lower.

Energy and livestock were the best performing components of the Index, while industrial metals and precious metals both declined in the quarter. Within energy, all sub- components achieved robust gains in the quarter. Within the agriculture component, unusual weather patterns, such as drought and high temperatures impacted agricultural output in many regions. Resulting in dramatic price movements in the quarter. Coffee and cocoa (not in the index) prices were significantly higher, while cotton and sugar prices were sharply lower. In industrial metals, all sub-components declined, with the biggest price declines seen in nickel and copper. In precious metals, gold and silver prices both fell in the quarter.

The U.S. stock market gained 2.75% in the fourth quarter, based on the large-cap Russell 1000 Index, bringing its return for the full year to 24.51%. The November election result removed a long-standing source of uncertainty for investors, and it raised hopes that government policy would shift in a more pro-growth, market-friendly direction. In addition, U.S. equities continued to attract investor capital as the nation’s economic growth remained well above that of its global developed-market peers. These factors helped propel stocks to a positive return despite indications that the U.S. Federal Reserve is unlikely to cut interest rates significantly in the year ahead.

Quarterly portfolio recap

The Columbia Commodity Strategy Fund’s institutional share class returned -0.21% during the fourth quarter. The strategy outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg Commodity Index which returned -0.45% over the same period. The fund’s outperformance against the benchmark can be attributed to sector allocation. Overweight positioning in energy was the primary driver of performance. Overweight allocations to softs and livestock also contributed to relative performance.

Slightly offsetting the above results was the fund’s security selection. Curve positioning in energy, grains and softs detracted from relative performance.

Additional details regarding the fund’s relative contributing and detracting sector exposures compared to the Bloomberg Commodity Index have been noted below:

Softs

  • Softs was the single largest contributing sector for the fund relative to the Bloomberg Commodity Index, driven primarily by an off-benchmark allocation to cocoa.
  • Coffee, cotton and sugar detracted from performance primarily driven from the fund's overweight position in sugar.

Industrial metals

  • Within industrial metals, security selection drove relative performance, led by curve positioning in copper and nickel.
  • Slightly offsetting these results were overweight allocations to zinc and lead.

Grains

  • The fund's curve positioning and sector allocation detracted from relative performance. This was led by the fund's curve positioning in corn. Overweight allocations to soybean meal and soybeans also detracted.
  • Slightly offsetting these results was curve positioning in soybean meal.

Energy

  • Negative results in security selection were the primary drivers to relative underperformance, while an overweight allocation to energy only marginally offset these results.
  • Curve positioning in natural gas and crude oil were the main detractors.
  • Marginally offsetting these results were overweight allocations to crude oil and natural gas.

Precious metals

  • Positive results were driven by curve positioning in both gold and silver versus the benchmark.

Livestock

  • Within livestock, overweight allocations to feeder cattle and lean hogs were the primary contributors to relative performance.
  • The fund's curve positioning in live cattle and lean hogs also aided performance relative to the Bloomberg Commodity Index.

Outlook

Heading into the first quarter of 2025, the primary themes we are paying attention to are weather, geopolitical risks and the uncertainty of Trump’s implementation of his fiscal policies, specifically tariffs.

As we begin a new year our expectation is that weather will continue to drive volatility in the commodity markets as the winter season brings near freezing temperatures boosting demand for heating products.

Geopolitical risk: the U.S. and UK imposed additional sanctions on Russia’s oil tankers, adding approximately 180 vessels to the sanctions list, bringing the total amount to more than 200 vessels. Most of this supply is going to countries who are now indicating compliance with the U.S. sanctions. This will result in lower crude supply and higher

prices. For Russia, the path forward is to either acquire non-sanctioned tankers to transport its oil or offer it at the imposed $60 price cap.

Lastly, potential Trump tariffs also threaten higher prices. Our team will continue to monitor these developments and more as we move into the new year.
